_meta.description: Known supply chain failure modes used by the challenger agent to stress-test recommendations.

failure_patterns.name: Bullwhip Effect Amplification
failure_patterns.description: Small changes in downstream demand get amplified into large swings upstream, creating costly overstock and stockout cycles.
challenge_questions: Does this recommendation change order frequency or batch sizes upstream in the supply chain?
Will this amplify demand variability for upstream suppliers?
Has demand signal sharing with Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers been considered?
failure_patterns.example_failure: Retailer reduces order frequency to cut PO costs, supplier overproduces to hedge, bullwhip amplifies upstream

failure_patterns.name: Single Source Dependency
failure_patterns.description: Concentrating supply in one vendor eliminates redundancy and creates catastrophic risk if that supplier fails.
challenge_questions: Is this recommendation creating or increasing reliance on a single supplier?
What is the supplier's financial health and geopolitical exposure?
What is the recovery time if this supplier fails or goes offline for 4+ weeks?
failure_patterns.example_failure: COVID-19: Companies with single-source suppliers for PPE had zero alternatives when Chinese factories shut down

failure_patterns.name: Safety Stock Erosion
failure_patterns.description: Cutting safety stock without improving lead time reliability or forecast accuracy leads to stockouts during any demand spike.
challenge_questions: Has supplier lead time variability (CV) been measured before proposing safety stock reduction?
What is current forecast accuracy and is it above 80%?
What is the cost of a stockout vs. the carrying cost saving from the reduction?
safe_conditions: demand is stable and seasonal pattern is known
failure_patterns.example_failure: Toyota lean inventory: 2011 Japan earthquake wiped out supply for key semiconductor components with no buffer

failure_patterns.name: Working Capital Trap
failure_patterns.description: Optimizing one part of the supply chain improves costs but locks up cash elsewhere, creating a liquidity problem.
challenge_questions: Does this recommendation shift cash burden onto suppliers who may not absorb it?
Could this trigger supplier financial distress or price increases at next contract renewal?
Has the full C2C cycle impact been modeled?
safe_conditions: large suppliers can absorb terms shift
failure_patterns.example_failure: UK retailers extending DPO to 120 days caused multiple small supplier bankruptcies, triggering supply disruption

failure_patterns.name: Demand Averaging Blindspot
failure_patterns.description: Using annual averages hides seasonal spikes and lumpy demand patterns, causing under-preparation for peak periods.
challenge_questions: Has seasonality been explicitly modeled, or is this recommendation based on annual averages?
What is the peak-to-trough demand ratio?
Has promotional uplift or event-driven demand been accounted for?
safe_conditions: weekly or monthly demand model in use
failure_patterns.example_failure: Grocery retailers optimizing on annual averages ran out of flour and yeast in March 2020 as COVID demand spiked 8x

failure_patterns.name: Geopolitical Supply Concentration
failure_patterns.description: High dependence on suppliers or manufacturing in a single geopolitical region creates systemic risk from tariffs, conflict, or natural disasters.
challenge_questions: What percentage of supply will be concentrated in a single country or region after this change?
Has tariff risk or trade war scenario been modeled?
What is the resourcing plan if this region becomes unavailable for 6+ months?
failure_patterns.example_failure: US-China tariff escalation 2018-2019: companies with over 80% China sourcing faced sudden 25% cost increase with no alternative

failure_patterns.name: IT Integration Underestimation
failure_patterns.description: Supply chain initiatives routinely underestimate the time and cost of integrating with ERP, WMS, or TMS systems, causing delays and budget overruns.
challenge_questions: Has an IT dependency assessment been done for ERP or WMS or TMS integration?
Are the required APIs or data feeds already available, or do they need to be built?
Has the implementation timeline accounted for IT change management and UAT cycles?
failure_patterns.example_failure: Most WMS and TMS implementations run 40-60% over timeline due to underestimated ERP integration complexity

failure_patterns.name: Supplier Capability Assumption
failure_patterns.description: Assuming suppliers can meet new requirements without validating their actual capability.
challenge_questions: Has supplier readiness been assessed for the new requirements?
Do suppliers have the systems, capacity, or financial strength to comply?
What is the fallback if key suppliers cannot meet the new requirements?
failure_patterns.example_failure: Walmart RFID mandate: most small suppliers lacked capability to comply, forcing retailers to absorb tagging costs

failure_patterns.name: Change Management Gap
failure_patterns.description: Supply chain process changes fail at the human layer as warehouse teams, planners, and procurement staff resist or misapply new tools.
challenge_questions: Has the change management plan been designed for frontline operators, not just managers?
What is the adoption risk if warehouse or planning staff revert to old processes?
Has the incentive structure been aligned to reward the new behavior?
failure_patterns.example_failure: 65% of ERP implementations underperform due to poor adoption by operational staff even when technically functional

failure_patterns.name: Cost-Only Optimization
failure_patterns.description: Recommendations driven purely by cost reduction without modeling impact on service level, resilience, or customer satisfaction often create hidden downstream costs.
challenge_questions: Has the service level impact of this cost reduction been modeled?
What is the total cost of ownership, including hidden costs of lower reliability?
Has resilience impact been explicitly scored?
safe_conditions: service level maintained at current or above
failure_patterns.example_failure: Boeing 737 MAX supply chain extreme cost focus led to quality control gaps that contributed to systemic safety failures
